title: The Private Life of Chairman Mao
description: The Private Life of Chairman Mao is a memoir by Li Zhisui. Banned for exploring Mao's private life.
descriptionParagraphs: The Private Life of Chairman Mao is a memoir by Li Zhisui. Banned for exploring Mao's private life.

The surviving record is interesting because it shows how even ordinary-looking books can acquire a charged political afterlife. As a memoir, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in China. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: Banned for exploring Mao's private life. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: The Private Life of Chairman Mao is a memoir by Li Zhisui. Banned for exploring Mao's private life.
banningParagraphs: The Private Life of Chairman Mao entered censorship debates as a memoir associated with controversy, publication history, and state scrutiny. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political sensitivity.

The earliest event currently captured here is 20th-21st century in China, where Chinese state censors and party authorities banned or suppressed publication. Banned for exploring Mao's private life. Banned for exploring Mao's private life.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
